documentTitle: Multithreading: Race Conditions, Mutexes, Deadlocks & Thread Pools
lede: Multithreading lets a single program run many execution paths (threads) at once, far faster than doing everything one at a time. But the moment those threads start sharing data, new problems appear. Try the four scenarios below — a race condition that corrupts data, a mutex lock that fixes it, a deadlock that freezes the whole program, and why real programs use a thread pool instead of spawning a new thread for every task.

race.title: The program starts with a single main thread
race.desc: A process starts with one execution path (the main thread). In shared memory there's a single variable called counter, starting at 0 — this is the variable two threads are about to fight over.

race.title: Two worker threads are created: Thread A and Thread B
race.desc: The main thread spawns two threads. Both run inside the same process, so both can read and write counter — with no rule at all yet about who's allowed to touch it, or when.

race.title: The scheduler gives Thread A the CPU first
race.desc: Thread A gets scheduled first and reads the value of counter (0) into its own register, getting ready to increment it. Thread B waits its turn for now.

race.title: Thread A's time slice runs out — the CPU switches to Thread B
race.desc: Before Thread A can write its incremented result back, the operating system switches the CPU over to Thread B. Thread A is paused mid-task, with the value 0 still sitting in its register.

race.title: Thread B reads, increments, then writes counter
race.desc: Thread B has no idea Thread A is paused. It reads counter (still 0), increments it, then writes the result back: counter becomes 1.

race.title: The CPU returns to Thread A — it resumes from a stale register
race.desc: Thread A picks up exactly where it left off. It does NOT re-read counter, since it's still holding the old value (0) in its register. It adds 1 to that stale value, then writes counter = 1 — overwriting Thread B's work.

race.title: The bug: one update vanished (lost update)
race.desc: Both threads added 1 to counter — the final result should be 2. But because each one read the old value before either wrote back, one increment simply vanished. This is a race condition: the program's outcome depends on the luck of CPU scheduling.

mutex.title: The program starts, this time with a mutex lock
mutex.desc: Same setup as before, with a shared counter = 0. The difference: this time there's also a mutex lock in play — a kind of door lock only one thread can hold at a time.

mutex.title: Thread A and Thread B are created, agreeing to share the same lock
mutex.desc: Both threads share counter just like before. The difference is that this time they also agree to use the same mutex lock before touching counter — a rule that simply didn't exist before.
fields: must hold the lock before touching counter

mutex.title: Thread A gets the CPU and immediately locks the mutex
mutex.desc: Before touching counter at all, Thread A locks the mutex first. Since the lock is free, Thread A succeeds. From this point on, Thread B isn't allowed into that same critical section until Thread A releases the lock.
fields: blocked, waiting for the lock to be released

mutex.title: Thread A reads, increments, and writes counter — undisturbed
mutex.desc: Since Thread B is blocked waiting on the lock, nothing else can touch counter while Thread A works. From the perspective of the shared data, this operation happens as if it were a single, indivisible step.

mutex.title: Thread A finishes, then releases the mutex lock
mutex.desc: Once counter has been safely updated, Thread A releases the lock. The lock is open again — and Thread B, which has been waiting this whole time, finally gets its chance.

mutex.title: The CPU switches to Thread B, which immediately locks the mutex
mutex.desc: Thread B, previously blocked, now gets the CPU and immediately locks the mutex for itself. Because the lock enforces ordering, it reads the value Thread A already updated (1, not a stale value) — increments it to 2, writes it, and releases the lock.

mutex.title: Correct result: both increments were recorded
mutex.desc: With the mutex lock, both threads access counter one at a time, never simultaneously. The final result matches what's expected: 0 + 1 + 1 = 2. The price is a bit of waiting — Thread B was briefly blocked — but the result can be trusted.

deadlock.title: Two separate resources, each with its own lock
deadlock.desc: This time there are two different resources — Resource X and Resource Y — each protected by its own mutex: Lock X and Lock Y. Thread A and Thread B will both need BOTH resources to finish their work.

deadlock.title: Thread A gets scheduled and locks Lock X
deadlock.desc: Thread A starts working: it locks Lock X to access Resource X. It succeeds, since Lock X was free. Thread A will also need Resource Y later to finish its task.

deadlock.title: Around the same time, Thread B locks Lock Y
deadlock.desc: Thread B runs a similar pattern but in the opposite resource order: it locks Lock Y first to access Resource Y. It succeeds too, since Lock Y was free at that moment.

deadlock.title: Thread A, still holding X, now asks for Y
deadlock.desc: To continue its task, Thread A also needs Resource Y — it tries to lock Lock Y. But Lock Y is held by Thread B, so Thread A gets blocked and waits, all while STILL holding onto Lock X (it never releases it).
fields: waiting on Lock Y, but still gripping Lock X

deadlock.title: Thread B, still holding Y, now asks for X
deadlock.desc: Thread B needs Resource X to continue — it tries to lock Lock X. But Lock X is held by Thread A, so Thread B also gets blocked and waits, all while STILL holding onto Lock Y.
fields: waiting on Lock X, but still gripping Lock Y

deadlock.title: Both wait on each other forever — the program freezes completely
deadlock.desc: Thread A is waiting for Thread B to release Lock Y. Thread B is waiting for Thread A to release Lock X. Neither will ever release what it's holding, because both are waiting, not working. This is a circular wait — the program hangs forever, unless something intervenes from the outside.

deadlock.title: How to prevent it: a consistent locking order
deadlock.desc: The root cause is a circular wait: A holds X and wants Y, B holds Y and wants X. The most common fix is forcing EVERY thread to lock resources in the same order — say, always Lock X before Lock Y, no exceptions — so this circular waiting pattern can never form in the first place.
fields: circular wait — two threads each waiting on the other's lock
a consistent lock ordering across every thread

threadpool.title: 8 small tasks arrive back to back
threadpool.desc: Imagine a web server receiving 8 small requests in quick succession. What's the best way to handle them concurrently without overloading the system?

threadpool.title: Spawning a new thread per task gets expensive
threadpool.desc: The most naive approach: create a brand-new OS thread for every incoming task. The problem is that creating and destroying OS threads isn't free — it costs time and memory. If tasks are small but arrive rapidly, the overhead of spinning threads up and down can outweigh the actual work.

threadpool.title: Instead: a fixed set of worker threads is created upfront
threadpool.desc: Rather than spawning a new thread every time, the system creates a small number of worker threads (say, 3) ONCE, upfront. Those same three will be reused across many different tasks, instead of being thrown away after just one.

threadpool.title: Incoming tasks don't get a thread right away — they queue first
threadpool.desc: Those 8 tasks don't each get their own thread immediately. They land in a task queue and wait their turn to be picked up by whichever worker happens to be free.

threadpool.title: Idle workers pull tasks off the front of the queue
threadpool.desc: The three previously idle workers each grab one task from the front of the queue and start working on it. The queue shrinks as tasks get picked up.

threadpool.title: Once done, a worker isn't destroyed — it grabs the next task
threadpool.desc: The moment a worker finishes its task, it isn't torn down the way it would be in the naive approach — it goes straight back to the pool, ready to pull the next task off the queue. The same thread gets reused across many different tasks.
fields: goes idle, immediately grabs a new task

threadpool.title: Thread count stays fixed, work keeps flowing through the queue
threadpool.desc: With a small, fixed number of workers and a queue acting as a buffer, the system can handle far more tasks than the number of threads that actually exist — without constantly paying the expensive cost of creating and destroying OS threads. This is why nearly every modern web server and runtime uses a thread pool.
fields: fixed (3), doesn't grow with the number of tasks
